mappings.rationaleAI: Oceans covering Earth's surface, with seawater chemistry, currents, and tides correspond to hydrologic sciences including oceanography (B.3); ocean circulation and waves in 20th-century hydrologic sciences (A.5.b).

The geologic sciences encompassing petrology, structural geology, stratigraphy, and plate tectonics (B.2); historical development from mineralogy beginnings (A.3.a) through 19th-century concepts (A.4.a) to 20th-century advances (A.5.a).
Earth's largest ocean with Mariana Trench and seamount chains relates to hydrologic sciences (B.3); charting ocean floors and bathymetry (A.5.b.iii).
Earth's internal structure, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and surface features connect to all Earth science disciplines -- physical geography (B.1), geologic sciences (B.2), and hydrologic sciences (B.3).
Radially layered interior inferred from seismic waves directly corresponds to advances in geophysics and seismology (A.5.a.iii); geophysics as a geologic science (B.2).

Physical oceanography, seawater chemistry, and marine biology correspond to hydrologic sciences (B.3); beginnings of oceanography as a discipline (A.4.b.ii); 20th-century hydrologic developments (A.5.b).
Study of Earth's lands, features, and spatial distribution directly corresponds to physical geography (B.1).
Mid-Atlantic Ridge, ocean floor bathymetry, and circulation correspond to hydrologic sciences (B.3) and charting of ocean floors (A.5.b.iii).

Development of paleontology and stratigraphy (A.3.a.ii); faunal succession and organic evolution (A.4.a.ii); advances in paleoecology and micropaleontology (A.5.a.v); paleontology as a geologic science (B.2).
Encompasses all Earth science disciplines -- geology, atmospheric science, hydrology, oceanography -- corresponding to the entire scope of history (A) and nature/methods (B) of Earth sciences.
Intercontinental sea with unique desiccation history and circulation relates to hydrologic sciences (B.3); study of tides and ancient observations (A.2.b.iii).
Monsoon-driven climate and unique upwelling patterns correspond to hydrologic sciences (B.3); study of ocean circulation, currents, and waves (A.5.b.iv).

Earth's 4.54-billion-year history with geological eons and mass extinctions relates to the concept of geologic time and estimates of Earth's age (A.4.a.v); historical geology and geochronology as geologic sciences (B.2).
Solid terrestrial surface including terrain types and geomorphological processes corresponds to physical geography (B.1); concepts of landform evolution (A.4.a.vi).
Darwin's contributions to the concept of faunal succession and organic evolution in 19th-century geologic sciences (A.4.a.ii).
Sea ice dynamics and Arctic oceanography correspond to hydrologic sciences (B.3); study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets (A.5.b.v).
Electrostatic atmospheric discharge and atmospheric chemistry connect to the 19th-century study of clouds, fog, and storms within atmospheric sciences (A.4.c.ii).
Ice sheet dynamics and ~70% of global freshwater as ice correspond to the study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets (A.5.b.v); glaciology within hydrologic sciences (B.3).
Enclosed freshwater bodies covering ~2% of Earth's surface, including limnology and thermal stratification, correspond to hydrologic sciences including limnology (B.3).
Natural freshwater channels with hydrology and geomorphology correspond to hydrologic sciences (B.3); theories of spring discharge and water balances in 16th-18th centuries (A.3.b).
Short-term atmospheric state and chaotic forecasting correspond to the study of weather and climate and the origin of synoptic meteorology (A.4.c.iii); advances in weather forecasting (A.5.c.ii).
Liquid precipitation and freshwater delivery correspond to theories of groundwater circulation and precipitation (A.2.b.i) and the study of clouds, fog, dew, and storms (A.4.c.ii).
Snowpack compacting into glacial ice relates to the study of glacier motion and ice sheets (A.5.b.v); snow as solid precipitation connects to the study of clouds and storms (A.4.c.ii).

Three fundamental rock classes connect to beginnings of mineralogy (A.3.a.i), classification of minerals and rocks (A.4.a.i), and experimental petrology (A.5.a.ii); petrology as a geologic science (B.2).
Long-term statistical patterns of meteorological variables correspond to the study of weather and climate (A.4.c.iii) and the classification of climate (A.5.c.v).
Broad history of science including Earth science developments from plate tectonics to paleontology provides overarching context for the history of Earth sciences (A).
The origin of the Nile and the cause of its floods was a specific topic of ancient Earth science inquiry (A.2.b.ii); the Nile as a major hydrologic feature (B.3).
Anthropogenic global warming and climate shifts relate to the classification of climate (A.5.c.v) and the study of properties and structure of the atmosphere (A.5.c.iii).
Islamic contributions to natural sciences including astronomy, physics, and chemistry connect to Earth sciences from antiquity through the 16th century (A.2).
Largest freshwater lake system with glacial origin, limnology, and ecology corresponds to hydrologic sciences including limnology (B.3).
Cloud classification and modulation of Earth's energy balance correspond to the study of clouds, fog, dew, and storms (A.4.c.ii) and advances in weather forecasting and cloud physics (A.5.c.ii).
Removal and transport of soil and rock by surface processes corresponds to concepts of landform evolution (A.4.a.vi) and geomorphology within geologic sciences (B.2).
Theory unifying continental drift and seafloor spreading directly corresponds to the theory of plate tectonics (A.5.a.vi); structural geology within geologic sciences (B.2).
Soil as a dynamic mixture at Earth's surface studied via edaphology and pedology connects to physical geography (B.1) and geologic sciences broadly (B.2).

Layer of mixed gases with stratification, greenhouse effect, and human impacts corresponds to atmospheric sciences across all historical periods (A.3.c, A.4.c, A.5.c) and the study of atmospheric properties and structure (A.5.c.iii).
European maritime exploration connecting isolated world regions provided impetus for geographical and oceanographic knowledge in the 16th-18th century Earth sciences (A.3).
Continental crust, tectonic plates, and supercontinents (Pangaea, continental drift) relate to plate tectonics (A.5.a.vi); geographic distribution corresponds to physical geography (B.1).
